chunks.title: OSI model and TCP/IP stack
chunks.text: The OSI model describes networking as seven layers: Physical (cables, voltages), Data Link (Ethernet, MAC addresses), Network (IP, routing), Transport (TCP, UDP), Session, Presentation, Application (HTTP, DNS). In practice everyone uses the TCP/IP stack which collapses Session/Presentation/Application into one Application layer. Each layer adds a header on send and strips it on receive — encapsulation.

chunks.title: TCP vs UDP
chunks.text: TCP is connection-oriented and reliable: it does a three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK), guarantees ordered delivery, retransmits lost packets, and does flow and congestion control. UDP is connectionless and unreliable: send a packet and hope. UDP is fast and stateless, used for DNS, video calls, online games where occasional loss is preferable to retransmission delay. TCP is used for web, email, file transfer where every byte must arrive.

chunks.title: IP addressing and subnets
chunks.text: An IPv4 address is 32 bits, written as four octets (e.g. 192.168.1.10). A subnet mask separates the network portion from the host portion. CIDR notation (192.168.1.0/24) says the first 24 bits are the network. Private ranges (10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16) are not routable on the public internet; NAT translates them. IPv6 uses 128 bits to solve address exhaustion, written in hexadecimal (e.g. 2001:db8::1).

chunks.title: Routing
chunks.text: Routers forward packets between networks based on a routing table that maps destination prefixes to next-hop neighbours. Interior routing protocols within an organization include RIP (distance-vector, simple), OSPF (link-state, scalable), and EIGRP. Exterior routing on the public internet uses BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) — autonomous systems advertise reachable prefixes to peers. BGP misconfiguration has caused multiple large-scale internet outages.

chunks.title: DNS
chunks.text: DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable names like example.com into IP addresses. It's a hierarchical, distributed database. Resolution starts at a recursive resolver, which queries the root servers, then the TLD servers (.com), then the authoritative servers for example.com. Records include A (IPv4), AAAA (IPv6), CNAME (alias), MX (mail server), TXT (arbitrary text used by SPF/DKIM). Results are cached based on TTL.

chunks.title: HTTP and HTTPS
chunks.text: HTTP is a request/response protocol over TCP. A request has a method (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE), URL, headers, and optional body; a response has a status code (200 OK, 404 Not Found, 500 Internal Server Error), headers, body. HTTP/2 multiplexes multiple requests over one connection. HTTPS wraps HTTP in TLS, which authenticates the server (via certificate signed by a trusted CA) and encrypts the connection. HSTS forces HTTPS-only.

chunks.title: Sockets and ports
chunks.text: A socket is the OS-level endpoint of a network connection, identified by (IP, port, protocol). A server listens on a well-known port (80 for HTTP, 443 for HTTPS, 22 for SSH) and accepts incoming connections. Each connection is a unique 5-tuple (src IP, src port, dst IP, dst port, protocol). Ports below 1024 are privileged on Unix systems. The socket API is the same on Windows and Unix (Berkeley sockets) — bind, listen, accept, connect, send, recv.

chunks.title: Network security basics
chunks.text: Firewalls filter packets based on rules — allow or deny based on source/destination IP, port, and protocol. NAT hides internal addresses behind a single public address. VPNs tunnel encrypted traffic over the public internet, giving remote users a private link. TLS protects data in transit. Common attacks: DDoS (flood the target with traffic), MITM (intercept and modify traffic — TLS defends against this), DNS spoofing, ARP poisoning.

chunks.title: ARP and MAC addresses
chunks.text: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) maps an IP address to a MAC address on a local network. When host A wants to send to IP B, it broadcasts 'who has B?'; the host with B responds with its MAC. A MAC address is a 48-bit hardware identifier baked into the NIC. The ARP cache stores recent mappings for a few minutes. ARP spoofing — replying with a fake MAC — is a common local network attack defended by static ARP entries or dynamic ARP inspection.

chunks.title: ICMP and ping
chunks.text: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) carries network diagnostic and error messages — destination unreachable, time exceeded, redirect. ping sends ICMP Echo Request and waits for Echo Reply, measuring round-trip time and packet loss. traceroute sends packets with increasing TTL; each router decrements TTL and returns 'time exceeded' when it hits zero, revealing the path. Firewalls often block ICMP, breaking these tools without breaking actual traffic.

chunks.title: DHCP
chunks.text: DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) auto-configures hosts joining a network with an IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, and DNS servers. The handshake is DORA: DHCP Discover (broadcast), DHCP Offer (server proposes IP), DHCP Request (client confirms), DHCP Ack (server commits). Leases expire and are renewed. Static reservations bind specific MACs to specific IPs. Rogue DHCP servers are a security risk.

chunks.title: NAT deep dive
chunks.text: NAT (Network Address Translation) lets many devices share one public IP. The router rewrites source addresses on outbound packets and tracks the mappings in a connection table to route replies back to the right internal host. PAT (Port Address Translation) uses port numbers to distinguish flows. NAT breaks end-to-end connectivity — incoming connections need explicit port forwarding. CGNAT (Carrier-Grade NAT) shares one public IP across many subscribers.

chunks.title: Subnetting practice
chunks.text: Subnetting splits a network into smaller subnetworks by extending the network portion of the mask. /24 gives 254 usable host addresses (256 minus network and broadcast). /16 gives 65,534. /30 (mask 255.255.255.252) gives 2 usable — typical for point-to-point links. Variable Length Subnet Masking (VLSM) assigns different mask sizes to different subnets to match their host counts.

chunks.title: VLANs
chunks.text: A VLAN (Virtual LAN) logically partitions a switched network so devices on different VLANs can't talk to each other at Layer 2 — even if they share the same physical switch. VLANs are identified by a 12-bit tag (4096 max) in the 802.1Q header. Trunk ports between switches carry tagged traffic for multiple VLANs. Inter-VLAN routing requires a layer-3 device. VLANs are used to isolate user, server, and management traffic.

chunks.title: Wireshark and packet analysis
chunks.text: Wireshark captures and decodes network traffic in real time. It parses every layer of the stack so you can see TCP retransmissions, TLS handshake details, HTTP headers, DNS queries. Display filters (http, tcp.port == 443, ip.src == 10.0.0.5) narrow the view. Used to debug slow connections, security incidents, protocol implementations. tcpdump is the CLI equivalent — capture to .pcap files, open later in Wireshark.

chunks.title: WebSocket protocol
chunks.text: WebSocket provides a full-duplex, persistent connection over a single TCP socket, after an HTTP-based handshake (HTTP Upgrade header). Unlike HTTP, either side can send messages at any time. Used for chat apps, live dashboards, multiplayer games — anywhere the server pushes updates. The fallback when WebSocket isn't available (corporate proxies sometimes block the upgrade) is Server-Sent Events or long polling.

chunks.title: HTTP/2 and HTTP/3
chunks.text: HTTP/2 multiplexes multiple streams over one TCP connection, eliminating head-of-line blocking at the HTTP layer (but not TCP). It uses binary framing, header compression (HPACK), and server push. HTTP/3 replaces TCP with QUIC (over UDP), eliminating TCP head-of-line blocking entirely and reducing connection-setup time (0-RTT for known servers). All modern browsers support HTTP/3.

chunks.title: QUIC
chunks.text: QUIC is a transport protocol over UDP that provides TCP-like reliability and TLS 1.3 encryption built in. It establishes a secure connection in 1-RTT (or 0-RTT for known servers), supports stream multiplexing without head-of-line blocking, and handles connection migration when a device changes networks. Google deployed QUIC across its services; it's the foundation of HTTP/3.

chunks.title: TLS handshake
chunks.text: TLS 1.3 handshake: client sends ClientHello with supported cipher suites and a key share for a Diffie-Hellman group. Server replies with ServerHello picking a cipher and its own key share, plus its certificate and a signature. Both sides derive the same shared secret from the DH exchange. Encrypted application data follows. TLS 1.3 cut the handshake from two round trips (TLS 1.2) to one, and supports 0-RTT for repeat connections.

chunks.title: Certificate authorities
chunks.text: TLS relies on a trust hierarchy: root CAs (built into browsers/OSes) sign intermediate certificates, which sign end-entity (leaf) certificates for websites. The browser verifies the chain — signatures, expiry, revocation status. Let's Encrypt issues free automated certificates and now powers a large fraction of the web. Certificate transparency logs publicly record every issued cert so misissuance can be detected.

chunks.title: Diffie-Hellman key exchange
chunks.text: Diffie-Hellman lets two parties agree on a shared secret over a public channel without ever transmitting the secret. Each picks a private value, raises a public generator to that value, and exchanges the result. Each multiplies the other's result by their private value to get the same shared key. ECDH uses elliptic curves for smaller keys and faster computation. DH provides forward secrecy — past sessions stay private even if long-term keys leak later.

chunks.title: Public key infrastructure (PKI)
chunks.text: PKI is the system of policies, software, hardware, and procedures that distribute and manage digital certificates. Components: CAs that issue certs, RAs (Registration Authorities) that verify identity, certificate revocation lists (CRLs) or OCSP for revocation checks, key escrow for recovery. PKI underlies TLS, code signing, S/MIME email, and document signing.

chunks.title: Email protocols
chunks.text: SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) sends mail between servers. POP3 downloads mail to a client and (by default) removes it from the server. IMAP keeps mail on the server and lets multiple clients sync, supporting folders. Modern mail relies on TLS for transport security and DKIM/SPF/DMARC to authenticate senders. Webmail bypasses POP3/IMAP entirely — browser talks HTTPS to the provider.

chunks.title: FTP, SFTP, SCP
chunks.text: FTP (port 21) is the classic file transfer protocol — separate control and data channels, plaintext (vulnerable). FTPS adds TLS. SFTP runs over SSH (port 22) and is the modern default — encrypted, single channel, works through NAT. SCP is a quick SSH-based copy command. For large datasets, rsync (over SSH) syncs only changed bytes.

chunks.title: SSH
chunks.text: SSH (Secure Shell) provides encrypted remote terminal access on port 22. Key-based auth (ed25519, RSA) is preferred over passwords. SSH tunnelling forwards local ports through the encrypted channel, enabling secure access to internal services. SSH agent forwarding (use with care) lets a remote host use your local keys. ssh-copy-id installs your public key on a remote host.

chunks.title: VPN protocols
chunks.text: OpenVPN is open-source, runs over UDP or TCP, uses TLS for key exchange. IPSec operates at the IP layer with two modes — Transport (encrypts payload) and Tunnel (encrypts whole packet). WireGuard is newer, simpler (only ~4000 lines of code), faster, uses modern crypto (ChaCha20, Curve25519). Most commercial VPN services use WireGuard or OpenVPN; enterprise often uses IPSec.

chunks.title: CDN (Content Delivery Network)
chunks.text: A CDN replicates static content (images, JS, CSS, video) to servers near users worldwide. The browser is directed to the nearest edge via geographic DNS or anycast. Benefits: lower latency, less origin load, DDoS absorption, easier global TLS termination. Cloudflare, Fastly, CloudFront, Akamai are major providers. Modern CDNs also run code at the edge (Cloudflare Workers, Lambda@Edge).

chunks.title: Anycast routing
chunks.text: Anycast assigns the same IP address to multiple servers in different locations; routers send traffic to the nearest one by BGP path metric. Used by DNS root servers (a single IP routes to ~1000 physical servers globally), CDN edge networks, and DDoS scrubbing services. Failover is automatic — if one anycast node goes down, BGP redirects to the next-nearest.

chunks.title: BGP path selection
chunks.text: BGP routers receive multiple paths to a prefix from peers. They pick one based on attributes in order: local preference (administrator-set, prefer customer routes), AS path length (fewer hops better), origin type, MED (incoming preference from neighbouring AS), eBGP vs iBGP, IGP cost to next-hop, router ID tiebreaker. Operators tweak these to control inbound and outbound traffic patterns.

chunks.title: Multicast and broadcast
chunks.text: Unicast sends to one recipient; broadcast sends to everyone on the local network (255.255.255.255); multicast sends to a group of subscribers (addresses 224.0.0.0/4). Routers join multicast groups using IGMP. Used for IPTV, software updates, stock tickers, and some routing protocols (OSPF). IPv6 deprecates broadcast in favour of multicast — all-nodes multicast replaces the broadcast use case.

chunks.title: IPv6 features
chunks.text: IPv6 addresses are 128 bits (340 undecillion total) written as eight 16-bit hex groups with shortcuts (:: for runs of zeros). Built-in features: stateless address autoconfiguration (SLAAC), mandatory IPSec support, no broadcast, no NAT required, simpler header. Adoption is happening gradually — major content providers serve IPv6 alongside IPv4 (dual stack). NAT64 lets IPv6-only clients reach IPv4 servers.

chunks.title: 5G basics
chunks.text: 5G is the fifth-generation mobile network, offering peak speeds of 10 Gbps, sub-1ms latency, and support for millions of devices per km² (IoT). Three deployment modes: low-band (broad coverage, modest speed), mid-band (balanced), and mmWave (huge speed but short range). Network slicing lets operators allocate dedicated virtual networks to different use cases (gaming, vehicles, factory automation).

chunks.title: Software-defined networking
chunks.text: SDN separates the network's control plane (decision-making) from the data plane (packet forwarding). A centralized controller programs forwarding rules into switches via APIs like OpenFlow. Benefits: programmability, faster reconfiguration, central visibility. Network virtualization runs many logical networks on shared physical hardware. Cloud providers run massive SDN deployments — AWS VPC is SDN under the hood.

chunks.title: Network congestion control
chunks.text: TCP must share bandwidth without overloading the network. Slow start exponentially ramps up the send window until congestion. After a loss, multiplicative decrease (cut window in half) and additive increase (slowly ramp up). Algorithm variants: Reno (classical), CUBIC (default in Linux), BBR (Google, models bandwidth and RTT instead of reacting to loss). Modern QUIC implementations let applications swap congestion control algorithms.

chunks.title: Bandwidth, throughput and the formula
chunks.text: Bandwidth is the maximum data rate a link can carry, measured in bits per second (bps, Mbps, Gbps). The formula is straightforward: Bandwidth = Data ÷ Time. For example, transferring 100 MB in 8 seconds gives 100 MB ÷ 8 s = 12.5 MB/s ≈ 100 Mbps. Throughput is the actual rate achieved (always ≤ bandwidth), reduced by protocol overhead, retransmissions, and contention. Bandwidth-delay product (BDP) = bandwidth × RTT, giving the amount of in-flight data needed to fully utilize a pipe — critical for sizing TCP windows on high-speed long-distance links.

chunks.title: Latency, jitter and packet loss
chunks.text: Latency is one-way delay; round-trip time (RTT) is there-and-back. Components: propagation delay (signal speed × distance), transmission delay (packet size ÷ bandwidth), queuing delay (waiting in router buffers), processing delay. Jitter is the variation in latency between consecutive packets — a constant 100 ms latency feels fine for streaming; jitter of 20-100 ms makes voice and video stutter. Packet loss occurs when buffers overflow or links flap. VoIP can tolerate ~1% loss with concealment; TCP retransmits but throughput craters under sustained loss. Tools to measure: ping (RTT), iperf3 (bandwidth + jitter), mtr (per-hop loss).

chunks.title: Quality of Service (QoS)
chunks.text: QoS prioritizes time-sensitive traffic over best-effort traffic on a shared network. Mechanisms: DiffServ marks packets with DSCP values that routers honour with priority queues; IntServ reserves bandwidth per flow (RSVP, used in MPLS networks). On a router, traffic is classified, marked, queued (priority, weighted-fair, low-latency), shaped (smooth out bursts), and policed (drop excess). VoIP and video conferencing demand low latency and low jitter; bulk downloads tolerate variable delay — QoS lets them share a link without the bulk traffic starving the real-time.

chunks.title: MTU and fragmentation
chunks.text: Maximum Transmission Unit is the largest packet a link can carry without fragmentation. Ethernet's default MTU is 1500 bytes; jumbo frames reach 9000. When an IP packet exceeds the path MTU, it must be fragmented (IPv4) or dropped with an ICMP 'fragmentation needed' message (IPv6 + IPv4 with DF flag). Path MTU Discovery probes for the smallest MTU on the path. Misconfigured firewalls that block ICMP break PMTUD, causing 'black hole' connections that complete the handshake but fail on large packets.

chunks.title: WiFi and wireless basics
chunks.text: WiFi (IEEE 802.11) operates on 2.4 GHz (longer range, more congested), 5 GHz (faster, shorter range), and 6 GHz (WiFi 6E onwards). Standards: 802.11n (WiFi 4, up to ~600 Mbps), 802.11ac (WiFi 5, gigabit), 802.11ax (WiFi 6, more efficient under load, OFDMA). Wireless faces hidden-terminal collisions, interference, and rapidly variable signal strength. Performance depends on signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), channel width, and the number of spatial streams. Wired Ethernet remains preferable for latency-sensitive workloads.
